title: Mr Lisa Goes To Washington

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening scene establishing Homer's character
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Homer's iconic catchphrase - high character integration and quotability, but routine usage

jokes.text: One million dollars. I'm rich!
jokes.setup: Homer finds what appears to be a million dollar check
jokes.punchline: His immediate assumption that it's real money
jokes.explanation: Homer's gullibility on full display - perfectly in character

jokes.text: See where it says 'Void, void, void' and 'This is not a check'? 'Cash value 1/20th of a cent.' 'Mr. Banker, do not honor.' - Shut up.
jokes.setup: Bank clerk lists increasingly obvious signs the check is fake
jokes.punchline: Homer's defiant 'Shut up' despite overwhelming evidence
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with absurdly obvious disclaimers, Homer's denial is perfectly stubborn

jokes.text: Every time you get a million dollars, something queers the deal.
jokes.setup: Homer's disappointment at the fake check
jokes.punchline: Implies this happens to him regularly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - Homer treats million-dollar windfalls as routine occurrences

jokes.text: I don't think real checks have exclamation points.
jokes.setup: Family discussing the fake check
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact observation about check authenticity
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor from Lisa - child's logical approach to adult problems

jokes.text: Marge, I never read a magazine in my life, and I'm not gonna start now.
jokes.setup: Marge mentions they got Reading Digest
jokes.punchline: Homer's proud declaration of ignorance
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer anti-intellectualism, sets up ironic reversal when he becomes obsessed with reading

jokes.text: Hey, a cartoon!
jokes.setup: Homer just declared he won't read magazines
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets interested in the cartoon section
jokes.explanation: Immediate contradiction of his anti-reading stance - Homer's drawn to the simplest content

jokes.text: Ain't it the truth? No, it's not the truth, Homer. It's well-documented that women are safer drivers than men.
jokes.setup: Homer agrees with sexist cartoon about women drivers
jokes.punchline: Marge corrects him with actual facts
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer/Marge dynamic - his casual bigotry vs her reasoned response

jokes.text: Oh, Marge, cartoons don't have any deep meaning. They're just stupid drawings that give you a cheap laugh.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to correct Homer about the cartoon's message
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses cartoons as meaningless while being in a cartoon himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke - Homer unknowingly describes The Simpsons itself, classic self-referential humor

jokes.text: Hey, you don't wanna eat? What you do, get one of them stomach staples?
jokes.setup: Homer reading instead of eating lunch
jokes.punchline: Barney's crude assumption about dieting methods
jokes.explanation: Typical Barney crudeness, in character but not particularly clever

jokes.text: As Tolstoy said in Quotable Notables... 'Give me learning, sir, and you may keep your black bread.'
jokes.setup: Homer suddenly becomes pretentious about reading
jokes.punchline: Quotes Tolstoy from a magazine compilation
jokes.explanation: Homer's overnight intellectual transformation is perfectly absurd - quotes serious literature from Reader's Digest

jokes.text: Simpson, eh? How very strange. His job description clearly specifies an illiterate.
jokes.setup: Burns observes Homer reading at work
jokes.punchline: Reveals Homer was hired specifically for being illiterate
jokes.explanation: Dark corporate satire - Burns literally hires for stupidity, perfect character moment

jokes.text: They take hundreds of magazines, filter out the crap... and leave you with something that fits right in your front pocket.
jokes.setup: Homer explains Reading Digest to Marge
jokes.punchline: His crude but accurate description of the condensed format
jokes.explanation: Homer's blunt assessment of Reader's Digest is surprisingly insightful in its crudeness

jokes.text: Homer, he obviously got out alive if he wrote the article. Don't be so-- Oh, you're right.
jokes.setup: Marge logically points out the narrator survived to write the story
jokes.punchline: Realizes her logic spoiled the suspense
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about narrative structure - Marge's logical mind ruins dramatic tension

jokes.text: That reminds me. 'Seven ways to spice up your marriage.'
jokes.setup: Marge wants to snuggle
jokes.punchline: Homer quotes marriage advice from his magazine
jokes.explanation: Homer's literal application of magazine advice to intimate moments creates awkwardness

jokes.text: 'You have a nice body... and if you'd like to see me in a costume, you have only to ask.'
jokes.setup: Homer reading marriage advice
jokes.punchline: Awkward magazine-scripted romantic dialogue
jokes.explanation: Cringe comedy from Homer's robotic delivery of romantic advice - kills the mood

jokes.text: Wow! Little meat loaf men!
jokes.setup: Marge serves dinner influenced by the magazine
jokes.punchline: Bart's excitement over novelty-shaped food
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag - child's delight in food presentation

jokes.text: Mom, it's a nice thought, but we both know that this is the pony to bet on.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Bart might want to enter the essay contest too
jokes.punchline: Lisa confidently dismisses her brother's chances
jokes.explanation: Lisa's precocious confidence and gambling metaphor perfectly capture her character

jokes.text: He'd say, uh... 'Think of a better opening.'
jokes.setup: Lisa struggles with essay opening about Ben Franklin
jokes.punchline: Self-aware critique of her own weak opening
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-writing joke - character critiques the exact line she just wrote

jokes.text: Do kids still use that word 'cool'?
jokes.setup: Marge trying to connect with Lisa about bike rides
jokes.punchline: Her uncertainty about current slang
jokes.explanation: Typical parent-child generation gap humor - Marge's dated references

jokes.text: Sweetheart, there's nothing I wouldn't do for that magazine.
jokes.setup: Lisa thanks Homer for driving her to the contest
jokes.punchline: Homer's selfish motivation disguised as support
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - reveals his true motivation while appearing supportive

jokes.text: six white stripes... seven red stripes and a hell of a lot of stars
jokes.setup: Patriotic essay about flag symbolism
jokes.punchline: Crude 'hell of a lot of stars' breaks formal tone
jokes.explanation: Humor from tonal inconsistency - formal patriotic language mixed with casual profanity

jokes.text: A little too good for an eight-year-old. Me thinks I detect the sickly scent of the daddy.
jokes.setup: Judge evaluates Lisa's sophisticated essay
jokes.punchline: Suspects parental assistance with pretentious phrasing
jokes.explanation: Clever phrase 'sickly scent of the daddy' - judge's pretentious language mirrors what he suspects

jokes.text: Could you touch your nose for me? Hmm.
jokes.setup: Judge questions Homer's intelligence
jokes.punchline: Basic sobriety test reveals Homer's mental state
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy - treating Homer like he's intoxicated rather than just stupid

jokes.text: Lisa, after meeting your father... I've decided to award you an additional five points.
jokes.setup: Judge interviews Homer to check for parental help
jokes.punchline: Homer's stupidity actually helps Lisa win
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - Homer's incompetence proves Lisa's talent is genuine

jokes.text: Wa-hoo! Who would have guessed... reading and writing would pay off?
jokes.setup: Family wins trip to Washington
jokes.punchline: Homer amazed that literacy has practical value
jokes.explanation: Homer's shock that basic education has benefits - perfectly captures his worldview

jokes.text: No, thank you. I'd rather push this button. Oops! - No! - We're all gonna die!
jokes.setup: Pilot offers to show Bart the cockpit
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately causes aviation disaster
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart destructiveness - escalates from cute tour to plane emergency instantly

jokes.text: Look, Marge, that guy has the same last name we do.
jokes.setup: Family arriving in Washington
jokes.punchline: Homer noticing Simpson on building (probably O.J. reference)
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor, likely pop culture reference that hasn't aged well

jokes.text: Oh, boo yourself.
jokes.setup: Homer boos at IRS building
jokes.punchline: Marge's mild rebuke and Homer's petulant response
jokes.explanation: Childish comeback typical of Homer - refuses to back down from anti-government stance

jokes.text: Shoe goes on, shoe goes off, shoe goes on, shoe goes off.
jokes.setup: Homer finds hotel shoehorn
jokes.punchline: Becomes obsessed with using it repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike fascination with simple tools - repetitive action becomes hypnotic

jokes.text: Hey, I call first bedsies. - Bart, there's no such thing as first bedsies. You just made that up.
jokes.setup: Bart claims first choice of beds
jokes.punchline: Lisa calls out his invented rule
jokes.explanation: Sibling dynamics - Bart's made-up rules vs Lisa's logic

jokes.text: What the-- D'oh! Stupid welcoming mint!
jokes.setup: Homer finds mint on pillow
jokes.punchline: Somehow injures himself with it
jokes.explanation: Homer can hurt himself with anything - even hotel amenities become dangerous

jokes.text: Wake-up call? It's 2:00 a.m. - Sorry, fatso.
jokes.setup: Homer gets wake-up call in middle of night
jokes.punchline: Operator's casual insult
jokes.explanation: Unexpected rudeness from service worker - breaks customer service expectations

jokes.text: That thing is really, really, really... good.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions she likes 'How to Increase Your Word Power'
jokes.punchline: Ironically uses repetitive, simple language
jokes.explanation: Clever irony - Lisa's vocabulary fails her when praising a vocabulary section

jokes.text: What does the 'I' stand for again? - Oh!
jokes.setup: Homer repeatedly asks about VIP badge meaning
jokes.punchline: Guide's exasperated response to his stupidity
jokes.explanation: Homer's repetitive stupidity wears down the guide - escalating frustration

jokes.text: 'On this spot, Richard Nixon bowled back-to-back 300 games.' - Yeah, right.
jokes.setup: Absurd historical claim about Nixon
jokes.punchline: Bart's skeptical response
jokes.explanation: Satirizes ridiculous presidential mythology - even Bart sees through the propaganda

jokes.text: Barbara Bush! Oh, you have those damn badges.
jokes.setup: Family walks in on First Lady in bathroom
jokes.punchline: Her annoyance at VIP access everywhere
jokes.explanation: Celebrity cameo with attitude - First Lady annoyed by tourist intrusions

jokes.text: Lousy, cheap country.
jokes.setup: Bureau of Engraving says no free money samples
jokes.punchline: Homer's ingratitude despite free trip
jokes.explanation: Homer's selfishness - calls America cheap while on free vacation

jokes.text: As you can see in our artist's rendition, it's full of old growth... just aging and festering away.
jokes.setup: Lobbyist pitches forest destruction
jokes.punchline: Describes natural forest as diseased
jokes.explanation: Environmental satire - corporate doublespeak portraying nature as unhealthy

jokes.text: Offer me a bribe? Oh, what is it?
jokes.setup: Lobbyist awkwardly approaches bribery topic
jokes.punchline: Congressman immediately interested in amount
jokes.explanation: Political corruption satire - congressman's casual acceptance of bribery

jokes.text: Tot shot always plays in the sticks.
jokes.setup: Congressman poses for photos with Lisa
jokes.punchline: Cynical political calculation about using children
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire - reveals calculating nature of political photo ops

jokes.text: If I ever vote, it'll be for him.
jokes.setup: Homer impressed by corrupt congressman
jokes.punchline: Admits he doesn't vote anyway
jokes.explanation: Homer's political apathy - empty promise from non-voter

jokes.text: Later, she appeared on the highly unpopular 75-cent piece.
jokes.setup: Lisa describing women's rights crusader
jokes.punchline: Absurd denomination for commemorative coin
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist historical detail about impractical coin denomination

jokes.text: 'I will iron your sheets when you iron out... the inequities in your labor laws.' - Amen, sister.
jokes.setup: Women's rights display with iron pun
jokes.punchline: Lisa's enthusiastic agreement
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting domestic iron with 'ironing out' problems

jokes.text: Well, let me put it this way, Jerry. Timber!
jokes.setup: Lobbyist asks about logging permit approval
jokes.punchline: Congressman enthusiastically approves destruction
jokes.explanation: Environmental destruction celebrated with logging call - dark political satire

jokes.text: Mr. Lincoln-- I need your advice. What can I do to make this a better country? Is this a good time to buy a house? I can't get my boy to brush.
jokes.setup: Lisa seeks wisdom from Lincoln Memorial
jokes.punchline: Other tourists ask trivial questions
jokes.explanation: Juxtaposes Lisa's noble question with tourists' mundane concerns

jokes.text: I never did anything important-- just the Declaration of Independence... the Louisiana Purchase, the dumbwaiter--
jokes.setup: Jefferson complains no one visits him
jokes.punchline: Lists major achievements then trivial invention
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation joke - major historical achievements treated equally with minor invention

jokes.text: Cool! A ruckus.
jokes.setup: Lisa's controversial essay causes outrage
jokes.punchline: Bart's excitement about chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart reaction - finds entertainment in sister's trouble

jokes.text: And this will be one nation under the dollar... with liberty and justice for none.
jokes.setup: Lisa's corruption-exposing essay
jokes.punchline: Perverts Pledge of Allegiance with money theme
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of patriotic pledge - sharp political commentary with memorable wordplay

jokes.text: And this will be one nation under the dollar... with liberty and justice for none.
jokes.setup: Lisa's corruption-focused speech
jokes.punchline: Subversion of Pledge of Allegiance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of patriotic pledge, sharp political commentary with memorable wordplay

jokes.text: A little girl... is losing faith in democracy. Good Lord!
jokes.setup: Aide reporting essay contest problem
jokes.punchline: Senator's horror at threat to democratic faith
jokes.explanation: Good political satire about priorities - corruption is fine, but lost faith is catastrophic

jokes.text: Teddy who?
jokes.setup: Lobbyist wants to drill in Teddy Roosevelt's head
jokes.punchline: Congressman doesn't know major president
jokes.explanation: Political ignorance joke - congressman doesn't know basic history

jokes.text: You work fast. I work for Uncle Sam.
jokes.setup: FBI arresting congressman immediately
jokes.punchline: Agent's patriotic explanation for quick action
jokes.explanation: Good government competence humor with patriotic twist

jokes.text: Your bosses? - Yep. All 250 million of them.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about FBI agent's bosses
jokes.punchline: Agent refers to all American citizens
jokes.explanation: Patriotic moment - public servant acknowledging democratic accountability

jokes.text: Where else but in America-- or possibly Canada-- could our family find such opportunity?
jokes.setup: Patriotic speech about American opportunity
jokes.punchline: Casual inclusion of Canada deflates rhetoric
jokes.explanation: Deflates American exceptionalism by casually including Canada as equally good option

jokes.text: I couldn't think of a nice way to say America stinks.
jokes.setup: Family asking about Lisa's harsh speech
jokes.punchline: Lisa's blunt summary of her diplomatic challenge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa deadpan - honest, intelligent, and economically phrased

jokes.text: I couldn't think of a nice way to say America stinks.
jokes.setup: Family questions Lisa's harsh essay
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact explanation
jokes.explanation: Lisa's brutal honesty delivered with child-like directness

jokes.text: I can't believe it. The system works.
jokes.setup: Corrupt congressman gets arrested after Lisa's speech
jokes.punchline: Lisa's renewed faith in democracy
jokes.explanation: Character growth moment - Lisa's idealism restored by seeing justice served

jokes.text: I can't believe it. The system works.
jokes.setup: Reading about congressman's arrest in newspaper
jokes.punchline: Lisa's surprised faith in democratic institutions
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's restored faith in democracy

jokes.text: I can't believe it. The system works.
jokes.setup: Lisa seeing corrupt congressman arrested
jokes.punchline: Renewed faith in democratic process
jokes.explanation: Lisa's genuine surprise that accountability exists

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Lisa doesn't win the contest
jokes.punchline: Homer's disappointed catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer catchphrase usage - callbacks episode opening

tmdb_overview: Lisa's essay wins the Simpsons a trip to Washington, D.C., where her faith in democracy is shaken by her unethical Congressman.

guest_stars.name: Jo Ann Harris
guest_stars.character: Minnesota Girl / Maria Dominguez / Paper Boy (voice)

guest_stars.name: Pamela Hayden
guest_stars.character: Bob Arnold's Secretary / Woman #1 at Lincoln Memorial (voice)

insight: Political satire carries a slower-burn episode with 61 jokes spread across its civic duty narrative.
